_comment: SFBB compiled content – Chilling and Cooking sections only. Inheritance rule: a safety point inherits the condition_id of its parent safe method unless it explicitly provides its own condition_id. Section condition_id applies to all safe methods under it, but each safe method can override.

safe_methods.safe_method_name: Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food
safe_methods._comment: Most safety points inherit chills_food. Only those with a more specific condition (e.g., displays_chilled_food, handles_raw_fish) are listed with their own condition_id.

safety_points.text: Certain foods need to be kept chilled to keep them safe, for example: food with a 'use by' date, food that says 'keep refrigerated' on the label, food you have cooked and will not serve immediately such as cooked rice and pasta, ready-to-eat food such as salads, cooked meats, sandwiches and cream-based desserts. If these types of food are not kept cold enough, harmful bacteria could grow.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Make sure you use food before its 'use by' date. Food with 'use by' dates, cooked dishes and other ready-to-eat food have a limited shelf life. Food cannot be supplied or served after its 'use by date'. For dishes you have prepared or cooked, use stickers or another method of labelling to keep track of when food should be used or thrown away. For guidance on how long to keep food, follow the manufacturer’s storage instructions on the product label. High-risk ready-to-eat foods should be kept for a maximum of 3 days in total (day of cook/opening + 2) unless you have evidence that it is safe to keep them for longer.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Follow the manufacturer's instructions on how to use fridges and chilled display equipment. It is important to use equipment properly to make sure food is kept cold enough. It is recommended that fridges and chilled display equipment should be set at 5°C or below. This is to make sure that chilled food is kept at 8°C or below. You should check the temperature of your fridges and chilled display equipment at least once a day, starting with your opening checks. To check that chilling equipment is working effectively, you can use a disinfected temperature probe to check that the food is kept at a safe temperature.To make sure equipment is working properly, check temperatures in-between packs of chilled food using a clean, disinfected probe thermometer.Some equipment will have a digital display or dial to show what temperature it is set at. You can use this to check the temperature of your equipment.If you do this, you should check regularly that the temperature shown on the display matches the temperature of a fridge thermometer or probe thermometer.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food > Prove it

safety_points.text: It is important to keep chilled food cold while it is on display to prevent harmful bacteria from growing in the food. Pre-cool the display unit before you put chilled food in it. Only display as much food as you think you will need. Display food for the shortest time possible. You could also use a 'dummy' portion for display (which will not be eaten) or use photographs to show customers what the food looks like. When you display cold food (e.g. on a buffet), you should use suitable chilled display equipment to keep it at 8°C or below. If this is not possible, there is a 4-hour rule exception: You can display food out of chilled storage for up to four hours, but you can only do this once. Make sure you know how long food has been on display or kept out of chilled storage. It is a good idea to label foods with the time they were taken out of the fridge or write this information in your diary so you can check the time easily. Food which has been displayed for less than four hours can be put back in the fridge and kept at 8°C or below until it is used. If it has been used for more than four hours, it must be thrown away.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Some foods require storage at temperatures lower than 8°C to keep them safe, so always follow the manufacturer's storage guidance.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Minimise the time chilled foods are kept out at room temperature during preparation. You can help do this by only preparing small batches one at a time.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: "Food that needs extra care" guidance: If you buy fresh fish make sure you store it between 0°C and 4°C
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilled Storage and Displaying Chilled Food

safety_points.text: Harmful bacteria can grow in food that is left to chill slowly rather than chilled as quickly as possible. If you have cooked food that you will not serve immediately, chill it as quickly as possible, then put it in the fridge. Chilling food is a prerequisite for freezing. Freeze hot food as soon as it has been properly chilled.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Freezing > Safety Points

safety_points.text: Options for chilling down food you can use are: divide food into smaller portions because Smaller amounts of food chill down more quickly. Cut joints of meat in half because Smaller pieces of meat will cool more quickly. Cover pans of hot food and move them to a colder area, stand them in cold water or use ice to speed up chilling to make the food chill more quickly. Stir food regularly while it is chilling down as doing this helps food chill more evenly. Spread food, such as rice, out on a tray as it will help it to cool more quickly. If you have a 'cool' setting on your oven use it to chill down food as it increases the air flow around it (The oven should be cold first). Consider using a blast chiller, which is specially designed to chill down hot foods quickly and safely.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food

safety_points.text: Avoid cooking large quantities of food in advance, unless you need to. Large quantities of food are more difficult to chill down quickly, especially solid food.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food

safety_points.text: When you have just cooked the food, use a probe to test its temperature. Then test the temperature again at regular intervals to find out how fast the food is being chilled down. Remember to use a clean probe each time you check the food.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food > Check it

safety_points.text: "Food that needs extra care" guidance: When you have cooked rice, make sure you keep it hot until serving or chill it down as quickly as possible and then keep it in the fridge. You can chill down more quickly by dividing it into smaller portions, spreading it out on a clean tray, or running it under cold water (make sure the water is clean and drinking quality).
source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Chilling Down Hot Food

safety_points.text:
Food should be thoroughly defrosted before cooking (unless the manufacturer's instructions tell you to cook from frozen or you have a proven safe method). If food is still frozen or partially frozen, it will take longer to cook. The outside of the food could be cooked, but the centre might not be, which means it could contain harmful bacteria.

The options to defrost food are:
- Ideally, plan ahead to leave enough time and space to defrost small amounts of food in the fridge. Putting food in the fridge will keep it at a safe temperature while it is defrosting.
- If you cannot defrost food in the fridge, you could put it in a container and then place it under cold running water. Cold water will help to speed up defrosting without allowing the outside of the food to get too warm. Raw meat and poultry (including large joints and whole birds) should not be defrosted under cold running water unless they are in a sealed container. For more information visit the FSA website.
- Defrost food in the microwave on the 'defrost' setting. This is a fast way to defrost food.
- If necessary, you could defrost food at room temperature. Follow the manufacturer's defrosting instructions. Food should be left out at room temperature for the shortest time possible. Ideally, defrost these foods in the fridge. Foods will defrost quite quickly at room temperature, but harmful bacteria could grow in food if it gets too warm while defrosting.
Keep meat/poultry separate from other food when it is defrosting, to prevent cross-contamination.
Once food has been defrosted, you should cook it until it is piping hot all the way through. After cooking, use the food immediately or chill or freeze it safely straight away.

Harmful bacteria could be spread, contaminating sinks, taps and surfaces. If you use the sink to defrost some food, make sure the sink is clean and empty. The sink should be cleaned and then disinfected after being used for defrosting. Cold running water will help speed up defrosting.

When you think food has defrosted, it is important to check to make sure. The outside may look defrosted, but the inside could still be frozen. Check for ice crystals in the food using your hand or a skewer. With birds, check the joints are flexible.
Food that must be kept frozen (e.g., ice cream) cannot be refrozen once it has started to defrost. You will have to use it immediately or throw it away.
Once the food is defrosted, its shelf life depends on the number of days left until expiry when it was frozen.

It is important to take care when freezing food and handle frozen food safely. Put frozen food in the freezer as soon as it is delivered. If frozen food starts to defrost, harmful bacteria could grow.

If you are freezing fresh food, freeze it as soon as it has been delivered or prepared. Freeze hot food as soon as it has been properly chilled. The longer you wait before freezing food, the greater the chance of harmful bacteria growing.

Divide food into smaller portions and put it in containers or freezer bags before freezing. Smaller portions will freeze (and defrost) more quickly. The centre of larger portions takes longer to freeze, allowing harmful bacteria to grow. Using containers and freezer bags prevents cross-contamination.
Food that must be kept frozen (e.g., ice cream) cannot be refrozen once it has started to defrost. You will have to use it immediately or throw it away.
'Food that needs extra care' guidance: Eggs, you can freeze them for later use if required.
'Food that needs extra care' guidance: If you buy frozen fish, then keep it frozen until you are ready to use it.
You can use the digital display, a dial thermometer or a probe thermometer to check that your freezer is keeping food at a safe temperature.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Chilling > Freezing > Prove it

safety_points.text:
It is essential to cook food properly to kill any harmful bacteria. If it is not cooked properly, it might not be safe for your customers to eat.

Do not forget that cooking does not remove allergens from food, so you need to handle food that contains allergens carefully.

It is also very important to handle ready-to-eat food carefully to prevent contamination with harmful bacteria. This is because it will not be cooked or reheated before serving.
Where appropriate, follow the manufacturer's cooking instructions for food products. The manufacturer has tried and tested safe cooking methods specifically for its products.

Preheat equipment such as ovens and grills before cooking as per the manufacturer's instructions, if available. If you use equipment before it has preheated, food will take longer to cook. This means that recommended cooking times in recipes or manufacturers' instructions might not be long enough.

Do not let raw food touch or drip onto cooked food, e.g. when adding food to a grill/barbecue. Never use the same utensils, plates or containers for raw and cooked or ready-to-eat food.

Raw food can carry harmful bacteria, which could spread onto cooked food and make it unsafe.

It is a good idea to fully cook poultry in an oven first, then finish it on the barbecue. This will make sure that the poultry is cooked thoroughly. The meat should be cooked to the safest temperature, and not be pink or red. Juices should be clear, with no pink in red meat.

Check that whole birds are cooked to a safe temperature in the thickest area, which is between the leg and breast. The meat should not be pink or red, and the juices should be clear.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text: If you are using leftover marinade as a sauce, make sure it is cooked until steaming hot. Marinades can carry bacteria from the raw meat or poultry if not cooked thoroughly.

Make sure liquid dishes, e.g. gravy, soups, sauces and stews, are simmering and stir them frequently. Check that they bubble rapidly and reach a safe temperature in several places in case of cold spots before serving.

This is to make sure the food is hot enough to kill bacteria. Stirring will help ensure the food is the same temperature throughout.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
If you serve beef or lamb rare (whole cuts such as steaks and whole joints only), make sure all of the outside surfaces are fully cooked, e.g. by sealing in a pan.

This will kill harmful bacteria on the outside of the meat.

Whole cuts of meat, such as steaks and joints, only ever have bacteria on the outside surface of the meat.

Pork, any rolled joints of meat and processed meat products, such as sausages and burgers, should not be served rare. They should be cooked to a safe temperature in the centre with no pink or red in them or their juices.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
Liver and offal, including dishes such as liver pate or parfait, must be cooked to a safe temperature in the centre of the meat and until there is no pink meat left.

Harmful bacteria can be found in the centre of the liver as well as the outside.
Turn the meat during cooking. This helps it cook more evenly and thoroughly.

Use a disinfected temperature probe to check that dishes are properly cooked or reheated.

Examples of safe time/temperature combinations for cooking include:
- 80°C for at least 6 seconds
- 75°C for at least 30 seconds
- 70°C for at least 2 minutes
- 65°C for at least 10 minutes
- 60°C for at least 45 minutes
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Prove it

safety_points.text: The largest piece of meat in stews, curries, etc., should be cooked to a safe temperature in the centre with no pink or red.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text: Check that combination dishes (e.g., containing meat and vegetables) are cooked to a safe temperature in the centre. If you are cooking a large dish or batch, check in several places.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
Check the fish is cooked to a safe temperature in the centre and the colour and texture have changed.

Some fish (e.g. tuna) may be served 'rare' as long as they have been correctly frozen beforehand to kill any parasites which may be present and are fully seared on the outside to kill any harmful bacteria that may be present. Further guidance is available at the FSA website.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Check it

safety_points.text:
Cook eggs and foods containing eggs thoroughly until they are steaming hot, or if serving eggs or egg dishes lightly cooked (e.g. soft-boiled or in fresh mayonnaise or mousse), either use:
• Pasteurised egg, or
• British Lion code or equivalent assurance scheme

Make sure you rotate stock and use the oldest eggs first. Use eggs within the 'best before' date. You can freeze them for use later if required.

Buy eggs from a reputable supplier. Store eggs in a cool, dry place.

Eggs can contain harmful bacteria. If you cook them thoroughly, this kills any bacteria.

The British Lion code or equivalent assurance schemes demonstrate that eggs have been produced in a safe manner and can therefore be eaten less than thoroughly cooked. Pasteurisation also kills harmful bacteria.

Harmful bacteria can grow in eggs that are not handled or stored correctly.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Foods That Need Extra Care

safety_points.text:
When you have cooked rice, make sure you keep it hot until serving or chill it down as quickly as possible and then keep it in the fridge.

You can make rice chill down more quickly by dividing it into smaller portions, spreading it out on a clean tray, or running it under cold water (make sure the water is clean and of drinking quality).

Rice can contain spores of a type of harmful bacteria that may not be killed by cooking or reheating.

If cooked rice is left at room temperature, spores can multiply and produce toxins that cause food poisoning. Reheating will not get rid of these.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Foods That Need Extra Care

safety_points.text:
Follow the instructions on the packaging on how to soak and cook dried pulses, such as beans.

Pulses can contain natural toxins that could make people ill unless they are destroyed by the proper method of soaking and cooking.

Tinned pulses will have been soaked and cooked already.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Foods That Need Extra Care

safety_points.text:
Make sure you buy shellfish (molluscs and crustaceans) from a reputable supplier. Keep the product label for 60 days after opening.

If you do not use a reputable supplier, you cannot be confident that shellfish have been caught and handled safely.

It is a legal requirement to keep labels for 60 days to trace suppliers, if needed.

Crabs, crayfish, lobster and scallops should be prepared by someone with specialist knowledge.

Some parts of these shellfish cannot be eaten, and some are poisonous, so it is important to know how to remove these parts safely.

Crustaceans and molluscs, such as prawns and scallops, should change colour and texture when cooked.

For example, prawns turn from blue-grey to pink and scallops become milky white and firm.

Langoustines (also called scampi or Dublin Bay prawns) are pink when raw, and the flesh becomes firm and pink-white when they are cooked.

Always follow the manufacturer's instructions for preparation and storage.

Before cooking mussels and clams, throw away any with open or damaged shells.

If the shell is damaged or open before cooking, the shellfish might not be safe to eat.

To check that a mussel or clam is cooked, make sure the shell is open and that the mussel or clam has shrunk inside the shell. If the shell has not opened during cooking, throw it away.

If the shell has not opened during cooking, the shellfish might not be safe to eat.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Foods That Need Extra Care

safety_points.text:
Make sure you buy fish from a reputable supplier.

If you buy fresh fish, make sure you store it between 0°C and 4°C. If you buy frozen fish, then keep it frozen until you are ready to use it.

Certain types of fish, such as mackerel, tuna, anchovies and herrings, can cause food poisoning if not kept at the correct temperature.
source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Foods That Need Extra Care

safety_points.text:
It is very important to reheat food properly to kill any harmful bacteria that may have grown since it was cooked. Remember, reheating means cooking again, not just warming up. Always reheat food thoroughly until it reaches a safe temperature in the centre.

Use a disinfected temperature probe to check that dishes are properly reheated. Examples of safe time/temperature combinations include:
- 80°C for at least 6 seconds
- 75°C for at least 30 seconds
- 70°C for at least 2 minutes
- 65°C for at least 10 minutes
- 60°C for at least 45 minutes
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Cooking Safely > Prove it

safety_points.text:
Make sure you use equipment that reheats or cooks food effectively and follow the equipment manufacturer's instructions.

Check equipment regularly to make sure it is working properly.

If the equipment is not suitable for reheating or is not used properly, the food might not get hot enough to kill bacteria.

Preheat equipment such as ovens and grills before reheating.

Food will take longer to reheat if you use the equipment before it has preheated. This means that recommended reheating times in recipes or the manufacturer's instructions might not be long enough.

If you are reheating food in a microwave, follow the product manufacturer's instructions, including advice on standing and stirring.

If you use a microwave to reheat food you have cooked, stir it while reheating.

The manufacturer has tested its instructions to make sure that products will be properly reheated. Standing and stirring are part of the process of cooking or reheating in a microwave and help make sure the food is the same temperature all the way through.

When food is microwaved, it can be very hot at the edges and still be cold in the centre -- stirring helps to prevent this.

When checking microwaved foods, test in a number of different areas in case of cold spots.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Reheating > Check it

safety_points.text:
Serve reheated food immediately or put it straight into hot holding.

If food is not served immediately, the temperature will drop and harmful bacteria could grow.
You should only reheat once.
Do not put food into a hot holding without reheating it properly first.

It is important not to overcook certain foods. Acrylamide is a chemical that forms naturally when some foods are cooked at high temperatures (above 120°C), such as by frying, roasting, baking, grilling, and toasting.

Legislation is in place to reduce acrylamide levels in food, as it has the potential to cause cancer in humans.

If you cook the following types of foods, you must put in place practical steps to reduce acrylamide.
- Raw potato products such as chips, French fries, other cut (deep-fried) and sliced potato crisps made from fresh potatoes, including potatoes that are deep-fried and finished in the oven.
- Bread products such as loaves, bread rolls and baguettes, toast and toasted sandwiches.
- Sweet bakery products such as cookies, biscuits, scones, gingerbread, wafers, crumpets.
- Savoury bakery products such as crackers, crisp bread, breadsticks.

Cook foods that form acrylamide when cooked above 120°C, to a golden yellow or lighter colour. Overcooking or burning certain foods can make them higher in acrylamide.

Some suppliers have produced colour charts to show which colour is best for certain foods to keep acrylamide levels low. You can ask your supplier whether they have these available. You do not have to use colour charts, but they can be useful for training your staff.

Where possible, set a timer to mark the cooking time. This could be on the oven or fryer, or you can use a separate timer.

This will remind you to remove foods at the right time to prevent them from overcooking or burning.

When buying raw potatoes, ask your supplier for advice on the best variety to use for the type of cooking you are doing.

Certain potato varieties are lower in natural sugars, and using these will help to keep acrylamide levels lower.

Store raw, unpeeled potatoes for frying, baking, or roasting in the fridge or a cool, dark place.

Storage of potatoes in the fridge doesn't increase acrylamide-forming potential when compared to storage in a cool, dark place. So, you can choose to store either in the fridge or in a cool, dark place.

When buying acrylamide-forming cooked products from a supplier, tell them you will not accept over-baked or burnt products.

Check deliveries and reject products that are over-baked or burnt, as these will have higher levels of acrylamide.

Ask your cooking oil supplier for advice on the best oil to use for the type of cooking you are doing.

Cooking foods in the right oil for the type of cooking will help foods to fry quicker and keep acrylamide levels lower.

When preparing foods that form acrylamide when cooked above 120°C, such as potatoes, cut them to similar sizes.

This will help all foods to cook more evenly.

These steps will remove excess sugars and help to keep acrylamide levels lower. Follow them where possible, when making home-made chips or cut potatoes that are going to be deep-fried:
- Soak (for 30--180 mins) in cold water after cutting. Rinse with clean water and drain.
- Soak for a few minutes in warm water. Rinse with clean water and drain.
- Blanch potatoes before cooking.

Where possible, and when the preparation process allows, when making bread or dough products, extend the yeast fermentation time. This will help to keep acrylamide levels lower in the finished product.

When baking bread and sweet or savoury bakery products cook to a golden yellow, or lighter colour. Use the lowest oven temperature possible for the food.

Baking foods to a golden yellow, or lighter colour, and at lower oven temperatures will reduce acrylamide levels.

When cooking foods such as toast and toasted sandwiches, do not over-toast or burn.

Cooking bread to a golden colour, or lighter, will help to keep acrylamide levels lower.

Where appropriate, follow the manufacturer's cooking instructions for food products.

The manufacturer has tried and tested cooking methods specifically for its products.

Deep-fry potato products, such as chips and French fries, to a golden yellow or lighter colour. The oil temperature for cooking should ideally be below 175°C.

Cooking to a golden yellow, or lighter colour, and deep-frying at lower temperatures will keep acrylamide levels low.

When deep-frying, take care not to overfill baskets. Fill the basket only halfway.

This will help the food to cook more evenly.

Keep cooking oil quality at its best by skimming often to remove crumbs and food particles left in the oil.

This will prevent crumbs and food particles left in the oil from burning and will keep the oil quality for longer.

Filter, change oils and clean cooking equipment as often as needed or as recommended by suppliers.

Reusing old, dirty oil and cooking equipment will increase the levels of acrylamide in deep-fried foods.

It is very important to keep food hot until serving to prevent harmful bacteria from growing.

If you need to keep food hot before serving, you should use suitable equipment.

It is difficult to hold food at a consistent, safe temperature without suitable equipment such as a Bain-marie or a Soup kettle.

Preheat the hot holding equipment before you put any food in it.

Putting food into cold equipment may not keep it hot enough to prevent harmful bacteria from growing.

Food must be cooked thoroughly and steaming hot before hot holding begins.

Hot holding equipment is for hot holding only. It should not be used to cook or reheat food.

If you offer food deliveries, make sure the food is kept hot or cold until it is collected by the delivery driver or customer. Also, make sure food is kept hot or cold during delivery until it arrives at the customer's home. It is a good idea to use an insulated bag or box to transport food and to make sure it stays covered until it is delivered.

If food is not kept either hot or cold, harmful bacteria can grow in it.

Using an insulated bag or box to transport food will help keep it at the correct temperature and in its best condition.

Hot food must be kept at 63°C or above, except for certain exceptions.

Remember to keep the food at a safe temperature until it is used.

Use a disinfected temperature probe to prove your method keeps food at a safe temperature of 63°C or above.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cooking > Hot Holding > Check it

safety_points.text:
When you display hot food, e.g., on a buffet, you should use suitable hot-holding equipment to keep it above 63°C.

It is possible to take food out of the hot holding to display it for up to two hours, but you can only do this once.

Food that has not been used within 2 hours should either be reheated until steaming hot and returned to hot holding, or chilled down as quickly as possible to 8°C or below. If it has been out for more than two hours, throw it away.

If you do take food out of hot holding to display, remember not to mix new food with the food already on display. This could lead to the older food being left out for too long.

When preparing and handling food, you should:
- Keep ready-to-eat food completely separate from raw meat, poultry, fish, eggs and unwashed vegetables.
- Make sure work surfaces, chopping boards, knives, etc., are clean
- Ideally, use separate colour-coded chopping boards and utensils for ready-to-eat food
- Keep ready-to-eat food covered at all times during preparation and storage
- If you are preparing both raw and ready-to-eat food, you should make sure, where possible, that this is done in separate clean and disinfected areas. If this is not possible, the surface and utensils used must be thoroughly cleaned and then disinfected between tasks.
- Make sure staff wash their hands thoroughly between tasks, especially when working with raw and ready-to-eat food. This prevents bacteria and allergens from spreading onto foods, surfaces, and equipment.

This protects food from harmful bacteria and allergens. This is especially important for ready-to-eat foods, which are not cooked or reheated before serving.

It also helps keep allergens from spreading.

Follow the manufacturer's instructions for storing and preparing the food, if available.

The manufacturer's instructions are designed to keep the food safe.

When preparing fruit, vegetables and salad ingredients:
- Peel, trim, or remove the outer parts, as appropriate
- Wash them thoroughly by rubbing vigorously in a bowl of clean water
- Wash the cleanest ones first

Wash your hands before and after handling fruit and vegetables.

If you have prepared vegetables with dirt or soil on the outside, clean them, then disinfect the chopping boards and work surfaces before preparing other food.

Dirt on vegetables and salad ingredients can contain harmful bacteria. Peeling and washing help to remove the dirt and bacteria.

Make sure you keep ready-to-eat food cold enough.

If these types of food are not kept cold enough, harmful bacteria could grow.

Do not use ready-to-eat food after the use-by date, if there is one.

You should never use food that has passed its 'use by' date because it might not be safe to eat.

For food you have prepared, or removed from its original packaging, use stickers or another method to keep track of when food should be used by or thrown away.

For guidance on how long to keep food once prepared or opened, follow the manufacturer's storage instructions on the original product label.

Ready-to-eat foods should be kept for a maximum of 3 days in total (day of cook/opening +2) unless you have evidence that it is safe to keep them for longer.
Cooked rice should only be kept for 1 day once prepared.

When slicing cooked meat, handle it as little as possible -- use clean tongs or slice it straight onto a plate. Hands can easily spread harmful bacteria onto food.

Follow the manufacturer's instructions when you clean the slicer. Meat slicers need careful cleaning and disinfecting to prevent dirt buildup and the growth of harmful bacteria, particularly on the slicing blade.

You should not use the same equipment, such as vacuum packing machines, slicers and mincers, for both raw and ready-to-eat food. These are complex pieces of machinery with many moving parts, and it is very difficult to clean them thoroughly, so bacteria from raw food could easily be transferred to ready-to-eat food.
additional_source_references: SFBB Pack > Cross-contamination > Separating Foods > Think Twice
